content.full_text:
California Fair Political Practices Commission

October 29, 1990

Beth Dacumos  
Assemblymember Richard Polanco’s Office  
State Capitol  
P.O. Box 942849  
Sacramento, CA 94249-0001

Re: Your Request for Advice  
Our File No. A-90-612

Dear Ms. Dacumos:

This is in response to your request for a confirmation of telephone advice on behalf of Debra Ortiz regarding the transfer prohibition in Section 85304 of the Act (the "Act"). Please note, that since the telephone advice was provided to you on September 25, 1990, the United States District Court issued an order in Service Employees International Union, AFL-CIO, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission, United States District Court, Eastern District of California, Case No. CIVS-89-0433, LKK-JFM (hereafter, "SEIU") which invalidates portions of the Act which were added by Proposition 73 in June of 1988. This letter has been drafted in light of the changes caused by the SEIU decision.

This letter confirms that your letter dated September 25, 1990 accurately summarizes the telephone advice I provided to you on that day concerning Ms. Ortiz’s performance of volunteer work for another candidate while employed with Assemblymember Polanco. As I stated in our telephone conversation, Section 85304 prohibits candidates from transferring campaign funds to other candidates. While this section was partially invalidated by the court’s decision in the SEIU case, under a stay issued on September 28,

__________________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ The court invalidated the contribution limitations of the Act with respect to all candidates other than legislative candidates. In addition, the court invalidated the transfer ban except as applied to valid per election contribution limitations.

1990, the section remains valid and enforceable with respect to legislative candidates.

However, the section no longer restricts the ability of a legislative candidate to transfer campaign funds to statewide candidates. Thus, while the transfer ban is no longer applicable to the situation you described, there is still a possibility that a contribution will result where a staff person of Assemblymember Polanco provides services to a statewide candidate on compensated time. As we discussed in our telephone conversation, however, so long as Ms. Ortiz does not provide services to the statewide candidate during times for which Assemblymember Polanco’s office compensates her, no contribution to the other candidate occurs. We suggest that accurate records be kept to make sure that Ms. Ortiz is not compensated by Assemblymember Polanco for services performed for the other candidate.

Finally, please be aware that campaign regulation in the California is in a state of flux. You or Ms. Ortiz should contact us if circumstances change, or significant time passes following the issuance of this letter.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please feel free to contact this office at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
